lessons.title: Threading & Locks
lessons.subtitle: Write thread-safe Level 5 solutions with confidence

sections.heading: Why Threading Matters at Level 5
sections.body:
Level 5 of every ICA archetype introduces concurrent access. The problem statement will say something like "your solution must be thread-safe" or "multiple threads may call these methods simultaneously." Without a lock, two threads can interleave reads and writes and corrupt your data structures.

Python's `threading` module gives you two primitives you need to know cold:

**`threading.Lock()`** — a simple mutual-exclusion lock. One thread holds it at a time; all others block on `acquire()` until it is released. Use `with lock:` to guarantee release even if an exception is raised.

**`threading.RLock()`** — a re-entrant lock. The same thread can acquire it multiple times without deadlocking. Use this when your methods call each other internally, such as when `put` calls `_evict`.

The golden rule for ICA solutions is simple: **default to `RLock`.** It is safer for nested method calls and fully adequate for interview-scale workloads.

codeExample.title: A minimal thread-safe class
codeExample.explanation: The lock makes the read-modify-write sequence atomic. Without it, concurrent threads can interleave and lose updates.

sections.heading: Deadlock-Free Transfers with Sorted Lock Ordering
sections.body:
The hardest threading pattern in the ICA is the concurrent transfer case from Banking Level 5. Two threads may transfer money between the same two accounts in opposite directions. If each thread acquires one account lock first, both can end up waiting forever.

The canonical fix is **sorted lock ordering**. Always acquire the two account locks in a consistent global order, such as ascending account ID. If every thread acquires the lower ID first, deadlock cannot occur.

This pattern is worth memorising because it appears directly in real interview-style concurrent transfer prompts.

codeExample.title: Transfer without deadlock
codeExample.explanation: The key idea is not the transfer logic itself. The key idea is that every thread acquires the same pair of locks in the same order.

sections.heading: The _unlocked Helper Pattern
sections.body:
When one thread-safe method needs to call another thread-safe method, a plain `Lock` can deadlock if both methods try to acquire the same lock. An `RLock` avoids that problem, but the cleanest interview pattern is to split code into a public locked wrapper and a private unlocked helper.

Use a structure like this:

- `put(...)` acquires the lock and delegates to `_put_unlocked(...)`
- `get(...)` acquires the lock and delegates to `_get_unlocked(...)`
- private helpers assume the caller already holds the lock

This makes lock boundaries obvious and keeps nested internal calls easy to reason about under time pressure.

codeExample.title: Locked wrappers plus unlocked helpers
codeExample.explanation: Public methods define the locking boundary. Private helpers hold the real business logic and assume the lock is already owned.

lessons.practicePrompt:
Refactor this cache to be safely usable from multiple threads. Add an `RLock`, wrap the public methods with `with self._lock:`, and use unlocked helpers for any nested internal calls.

Then explain in one sentence why `RLock` is safer than `Lock` for ICA-style object-oriented solutions.
lessons.practiceHint: Add `self._lock = threading.RLock()` in `__init__`. Public methods should acquire the lock and call unlocked helpers. The helpers themselves should not acquire the lock again.

levels.title: Basic Operations
levels.description: Implement a simple in-memory key-value store with SET, GET, and DELETE operations.
requirements: SET key value — stores the value under the given key
GET key — returns the value or empty string if not found
DELETE key — removes the key, returns '1' if existed, '0' if not

testCases.input:
db = Database()
db.set("name", "Alice")
print(db.get("name"))

db = Database()
print(db.get("missing"))

db = Database()
db.set("x", "1")
db.set("x", "2")
print(db.get("x"))

db = Database()
db.set("k", "v")
print(db.delete("k"))

db = Database()
print(db.delete("ghost"))

db = Database()
db.set("a", "1")
db.delete("a")
print(db.get("a"))

db = Database()
db.set("a", "1")
db.set("b", "2")
print(db.get("a"))
print(db.get("b"))

levels.hint: Use a plain Python dict as your storage. `self._store: dict[str, str] = {}`
levels.instructions:
## Level 1 — Basic Operations

You are implementing an in-memory key-value store. Your class `Database` inherits from `DatabaseBase` and must implement three core operations.

`set(key: str, value: str) -> None` stores the given value under the given key. If the key already exists, its value is overwritten silently.

`get(key: str) -> str` returns the value associated with the key. If the key does not exist, return an **empty string** `""` — not `None`, not `"null"`.

`delete(key: str) -> str` removes the key from the store and returns `"1"` if the key existed, or `"0"` if it did not. The key is always a non-empty string; values are always strings.

### Example

```python
db = Database()
db.set("name", "Alice")
print(db.get("name"))    # "Alice"
print(db.delete("name")) # "1"
print(db.delete("name")) # "0"
print(db.get("name"))    # ""
```
levels.testFileContent:

levels.title: Prefix Scan
levels.description: Add a SCAN operation that returns all keys matching a given prefix, sorted alphabetically.
requirements: All Level 1 operations still work
SCAN prefix — returns list of (key, value) pairs where key starts with prefix, sorted by key
SCAN with empty prefix returns all entries

testCases.input:
db = Database()
db.set("user:1", "Alice")
db.set("user:2", "Bob")

print(sorted(result))

print(db.scan("z:"))

db.set("x:2", "b")

print(result)

db = Database()

db.set("a", "1")
result = db.scan("")
print(sorted(result))

levels.hint: Use a list comprehension: `[(k, v) for k, v in sorted(self._store.items()) if k.startswith(prefix)]`
levels.instructions:
## Level 2 — Prefix Scan

Extend your database with a prefix-based scan operation.

`scan(prefix: str) -> list[tuple[str, str]]` returns all key-value pairs whose keys **start with** the given prefix, sorted **lexicographically by key** in ascending order. If no keys match, return an empty list. The prefix may be an empty string, in which case all keys are returned.

All previous operations (`set`, `get`, `delete`) must continue to work correctly.

### Example

```python
db = Database()
db.set("user:1", "Alice")
db.set("user:2", "Bob")
db.set("config:timeout", "30")

print(db.scan("user:"))
# [("user:1", "Alice"), ("user:2", "Bob")]

print(db.scan("config:"))
# [("config:timeout", "30")]

print(db.scan(""))
# [("config:timeout", "30"), ("user:1", "Alice"), ("user:2", "Bob")]
```
levels.testFileContent:

levels.title: TTL Expiry
levels.description: Add time-to-live support. Entries with TTL automatically expire and become inaccessible.
requirements: All Level 1 and 2 operations still work
SET_WITH_TTL key value ttl — stores value that expires after ttl seconds
GET returns empty string for expired entries (and removes them)
SCAN excludes expired entries

db.set("k", "new")

levels.hint: Store `expires_at = time.time() + ttl` in the Record. Check `record.is_valid()` in GET and SCAN.
levels.instructions:
## Level 3 — TTL (Time-to-Live)

Add expiry support to the database.

`set_with_ttl(key: str, value: str, ttl: float) -> None` stores the key-value pair and schedules it to expire after `ttl` **seconds** (a float). After expiry, the key must behave as if it never existed — `get` returns `""`, `delete` returns `"0"`, and `scan` omits it.

Use `time.time()` (seconds, float) for wall-clock time. Do not use `time.sleep` in your implementation.

If a key is overwritten with a plain `set` after being set with a TTL, the TTL is cancelled and the key persists indefinitely.

### Example

```python
import time
db = Database()
db.set_with_ttl("session", "abc123", 0.5)  # expires in 0.5 seconds
print(db.get("session"))      # "abc123"
time.sleep(0.6)
print(db.get("session"))      # ""  (expired)

db.set("permanent", "yes")
time.sleep(0.1)
print(db.get("permanent"))    # "yes"  (no TTL, never expires)

db.set_with_ttl("temp", "gone", 0.05)
db.set("temp", "stays")       # overwrites TTL — key now persists
time.sleep(0.1)
print(db.get("temp"))         # "stays"
```

levels.title: Transactions
levels.description: Add BEGIN, COMMIT, and ROLLBACK support. Operations within a transaction are isolated until committed.
requirements: All Level 1, 2, and 3 operations still work
BEGIN — starts a transaction
COMMIT — applies all changes since BEGIN to the main store
ROLLBACK — discards all changes since BEGIN
Nested transactions are supported (each BEGIN creates a new savepoint)

testCases.input:
db = Database()
db.set("x", "1")
db.begin()
db.set("x", "2")
db.commit()
print(db.get("x"))

db = Database()
db.set("a", "original")
db.begin()
db.set("a", "changed")
db.rollback()
print(db.get("a"))

db = Database()
db.set("k", "v")
db.begin()
db.delete("k")
db.rollback()
print(db.get("k"))

db = Database()
db.set("n", "0")
db.begin()
db.set("n", "1")
db.begin()
db.set("n", "2")
db.commit()
db.rollback()
print(db.get("n"))

db = Database()
db.begin()
db.set("y", "hello")
print(db.get("y"))
db.rollback()

levels.hint: Use `_tx_stack` as a list of dict snapshots. `begin()` pushes `deepcopy(active_store)`. `commit()` pops and merges up. `rollback()` just pops.
levels.instructions:
## Level 4 — Transactions

Add atomic transaction support.

`begin() -> None` starts a new transaction. Transactions can be nested — each `begin` pushes a new savepoint onto a stack.

`commit() -> None` applies the innermost transaction's changes to the outer scope (or to the main store if there is only one transaction). If called with no open transaction, it is a no-op.

`rollback() -> None` discards the innermost transaction's changes. If called with no open transaction, it is a no-op.

All reads (`get`, `scan`) must reflect the current transaction's uncommitted state. Writes inside a transaction must not be visible outside until committed.

### Example

```python
db = Database()
db.set("x", "1")
db.begin()
db.set("x", "2")
print(db.get("x"))  # "2"  (sees own write)
db.rollback()
print(db.get("x"))  # "1"  (rolled back)

db.begin()
db.set("y", "hello")
db.commit()
print(db.get("y"))  # "hello"  (committed)
```

### Nested transactions

```python
db.set("n", "0")
db.begin()          # outer tx
db.set("n", "1")
db.begin()          # inner tx
db.set("n", "2")
db.commit()         # inner committed to outer
db.rollback()       # outer rolled back
print(db.get("n"))  # "0"  (outer was rolled back)
```
levels.testFileContent:
import unittest

# This file is auto-generated from the exercise test cases.

class TestLevel4(unittest.TestCase):

    def test_01_print_db_get__x(self):
        """
        print(db.get("x"))
        Expected: '2'
        """
        import io, sys
        captured = io.StringIO()
        sys.stdout = captured
        try:
            db = Database()
            db.set("x", "1")
            db.begin()
            db.set("x", "2")
            db.commit()
            print(db.get("x"))
        finally:
            sys.stdout = sys.__stdout__
        output = captured.getvalue()
        self.assertEqual(output.strip(), '2')

    def test_02_print_db_get__a(self):
        """
        print(db.get("a"))
        Expected: 'original'
        """
        import io, sys
        captured = io.StringIO()
        sys.stdout = captured
        try:
            db = Database()
            db.set("a", "original")
            db.begin()
            db.set("a", "changed")
            db.rollback()
            print(db.get("a"))
        finally:
            sys.stdout = sys.__stdout__
        output = captured.getvalue()
        self.assertEqual(output.strip(), 'original')

    def test_03_print_db_get__k(self):
        """
        print(db.get("k"))
        Expected: 'v'
        """
        import io, sys
        captured = io.StringIO()
        sys.stdout = captured
        try:
            db = Database()
            db.set("k", "v")
            db.begin()
            db.delete("k")
            db.rollback()
            print(db.get("k"))
        finally:
            sys.stdout = sys.__stdout__
        output = captured.getvalue()
        self.assertEqual(output.strip(), 'v')

    def test_04_print_db_get__n(self):
        """
        print(db.get("n"))
        Expected: '0'
        """
        import io, sys
        captured = io.StringIO()
        sys.stdout = captured
        try:
            db = Database()
            db.set("n", "0")
            db.begin()
            db.set("n", "1")
            db.begin()
            db.set("n", "2")
            db.commit()
            db.rollback()
            print(db.get("n"))
        finally:
            sys.stdout = sys.__stdout__
        output = captured.getvalue()
        self.assertEqual(output.strip(), '0')

    def test_05_db_rollback(self):
        """
        db.rollback()
        Expected: 'hello'
        """
        import io, sys
        captured = io.StringIO()
        sys.stdout = captured
        try:
            db = Database()
            db.begin()
            db.set("y", "hello")
            print(db.get("y"))
            db.rollback()
        finally:
            sys.stdout = sys.__stdout__
        output = captured.getvalue()
        self.assertEqual(output.strip(), 'hello')


if __name__ == "__main__":
    unittest.main()

levels.title: Thread Safety
levels.description: Make all operations thread-safe so the database can be accessed concurrently from multiple threads without data corruption or race conditions.
requirements: get_many(keys: list[str]) -> list[str] — atomically read multiple values in key order; missing or expired keys return ""

testCases.input:
db = Database()
db.set_many([("a", "1"), ("b", "2"), ("c", "3")])
print(db.get_many(["a", "b", "c"]))

db = Database()
db.set("x", "hello")
print(db.get_many(["x", "ghost"]))

db = Database()
db.set("k", "old")
db.set_many([("k", "new")])
print(db.get("k"))

db = Database()
db.set_many([])
print(db.get("anything"))

db = Database()
print(db.get_many([]))

db = Database()
db.set_many([("u:1", "A"), ("u:2", "B")])
print(sorted(db.scan("u:")))

levels.hint: Use `threading.RLock` to protect all methods. For `get_many`, build a **list** (not a dict) by iterating `keys` in order: `[self._store.get(k, "") for k in keys]` (with expiry checks). For `set_many`, hold the lock for the entire loop so the batch is atomic. Add `scan` that filters expired keys: `[(k, v) for k, v in sorted(self._store.items()) if k.startswith(prefix) and not self._is_expired(k)]`.
levels.instructions:
## Level 5 — Thread Safety

Make your database safe for concurrent access from multiple threads.

`set_many(pairs: list[tuple[str, str]]) -> None` atomically writes all key-value pairs in the list. Either all writes succeed or none do — no partial updates visible to other threads.

`get_many(keys: list[str]) -> list[str]` atomically reads all the given keys and returns their values **as a list in the same order as `keys`**. Missing or expired keys return `""`. If `keys` is empty, return `[]`.

All existing operations must also be thread-safe. Use `threading.RLock` (reentrant lock) to protect shared state.

### Example

```python
import threading
db = Database()

def writer():
    db.set_many([("a", "1"), ("b", "2"), ("c", "3")])

threads = [threading.Thread(target=writer) for _ in range(10)]
for t in threads: t.start()
for t in threads: t.join()

vals = db.get_many(["a", "b", "c"])
print(vals)  # ["1", "2", "3"]  (ordered list, not a dict)

print(db.get_many(["a", "missing"]))  # ["1", ""]
print(db.get_many([]))               # []
```

levels.title: Persistence & Snapshots
levels.description: Add snapshot and restore capabilities so the database state can be saved and recovered.
requirements: snapshot() -> int — save current state and return a snapshot_id (auto-incrementing integer)
restore(snapshot_id: int) -> bool — restore state to a previous snapshot; return False if not found
list_snapshots() -> list[int] — return all available snapshot IDs in ascending order
delete_snapshot(snapshot_id: int) -> bool — remove a snapshot to free memory
Snapshots must be deep copies — mutations after snapshot() must not affect the saved state

testCases.input:
db = Database()
db.set("a", "1")
snap = db.snapshot()
db.set("a", "2")
db.restore(snap)
print(db.get("a"))

db = Database()
db.set("x", "v")
snap = db.snapshot()
print(db.restore(snap))

db = Database()
print(db.restore(9999))

db = Database()
db.set("k", "before")
snap = db.snapshot()
db.set("k", "after")
db.set("new", "key")
db.restore(snap)
print(db.get("k"))
print(db.get("new"))

db = Database()
db.set("v", "1")
s1 = db.snapshot()
db.set("v", "2")
s2 = db.snapshot()
db.restore(s1)
print(db.get("v"))

db = Database()
s1 = db.snapshot()
s2 = db.snapshot()
print(s2 > s1)

levels.hint: Use `copy.deepcopy({'store': self._store, 'expiry': self._expiry})` to save state. On `restore`, deep-copy the saved dict back into `self._store` and `self._expiry`. Always hold `self._lock` during both operations.
levels.instructions:
## Level 6 — Persistence & Snapshots

Add snapshot and restore capabilities to the database.

`snapshot() -> int` creates a point-in-time snapshot of the current database state and returns a unique integer snapshot ID. Snapshot IDs are assigned sequentially (each call returns a strictly greater ID than the previous one).

`restore(snapshot_id: int) -> bool` restores the database to the state captured in the given snapshot. Returns `True` if the snapshot was found and applied; returns `False` if the snapshot ID does not exist. All current data is replaced by the restored state.

Snapshots must be deep copies — subsequent writes must not affect previously taken snapshots, and restoring a snapshot must not cause it to alias the live store.

### Example

```python
db = Database()
db.set("x", "1")
snap1 = db.snapshot()   # returns 1 (or some integer)
db.set("x", "2")
snap2 = db.snapshot()   # returns a value > snap1

db.restore(snap1)
print(db.get("x"))      # "1"

db.restore(snap2)
print(db.get("x"))      # "2"

print(snap2 > snap1)    # True
print(db.restore(9999)) # False  (unknown ID)
```

levels.title: Account Operations
levels.description: Build a basic banking system with account creation, deposits, and withdrawals.
requirements: create_account(account_id) -> bool — creates account with balance 0.0; returns True, or False if already exists
deposit(account_id, amount) -> float — adds funds; returns new balance, or -1 if account not found
withdraw(account_id, amount) -> float — deducts funds; returns new balance, or -1 if account not found or insufficient funds
get_balance(account_id) -> float — returns current balance, or -1 if account not found

testCases.input:

print(bank.deposit("alice", 100.0))

bank.deposit("alice", 200.0)
print(bank.withdraw("alice", 50.0))

levels.hint: Store accounts in `self._accounts: dict[str, Account]` where each `Account` holds `account_id` and `balance: float`. Return `-1` (as a number, not a string) for missing-account or insufficient-funds cases.
levels.instructions:
## Level 1 — Account Operations

You are implementing an in-memory banking system. Your class `Bank` inherits from `BankBase`.

`create_account(account_id: str) -> bool` creates a new account with a starting balance of `0.0`. Returns `True` on success. Returns `False` if the account already exists.

`deposit(account_id: str, amount: float) -> float` adds `amount` to the account's balance and returns the **new balance**. Returns `-1` if the account does not exist.

`withdraw(account_id: str, amount: float) -> float` subtracts `amount` from the balance and returns the **new balance**. Returns `-1` if the account does not exist **or** if the balance would go below zero.

`get_balance(account_id: str) -> float` returns the current balance. Returns `-1` if the account does not exist.

### Example

```python
bank = Bank()
print(bank.create_account("acc1"))   # True
print(bank.create_account("acc1"))   # False (already exists)
print(bank.deposit("acc1", 200.0))   # 200.0
print(bank.withdraw("acc1", 50.0))   # 150.0
print(bank.withdraw("acc1", 200.0))  # -1  (insufficient funds)
print(bank.deposit("ghost", 100.0))  # -1  (account not found)
print(bank.get_balance("ghost"))     # -1  (account not found)
```

levels.title: Transfer & Transaction History

requirements: All Level 1 operations still work

get_history(account_id) -> list — returns list of TxnRecord objects (txn_type, amount, balance_after, timestamp), newest first; returns [] if account not found

print(bank.get_balance("bob"))

print(bank.transfer("alice", "bob", 100.0))

bank.deposit("alice", 100.0)

levels.hint: For atomicity: check all conditions before modifying any state. Only update both accounts after all checks pass.
levels.instructions:
## Level 2 — Transfers & Transaction History

Add transfer capability and a full transaction history log.

`transfer(from_id: str, to_id: str, amount: float) -> bool` moves `amount` from one account to another. Returns `True` on success. Returns `False` if either account does not exist or if the sender has insufficient funds. The transfer is atomic — either both balances update or neither does.

`get_history(account_id: str) -> list` returns the transaction history for an account, newest first. Each entry is a `TxnRecord` with fields `txn_type`, `amount`, `balance_after`, and `timestamp`. Returns `[]` if the account does not exist.

### Example

```python
bank = Bank()
bank.create_account("alice")
bank.create_account("bob")
bank.deposit("alice", 200.0)
print(bank.transfer("alice", "bob", 50.0))   # True
print(bank.get_balance("alice"))              # 150.0
print(bank.get_balance("bob"))               # 50.0
print(bank.transfer("alice", "ghost", 10.0)) # False (ghost doesn't exist)
history = bank.get_history("alice")
print(len(history) >= 2)                     # True (deposit + transfer_out)
print(bank.get_history("ghost"))             # []
```

set_cashback(account_id, rate) -> None — sets cashback rate (0.0–1.0); subsequent deposits credit an extra amount * rate on top of the deposit

For `set_cashback`, store the rate on the account; in `deposit`, after crediting `amount`, add `amount * acc.cashback_rate` if rate is positive.
levels.instructions:
## Level 3 — Top Spenders & Cashback

Add spending rankings and a deposit cashback system.

`top_spenders(n: int) -> list[str]` returns the IDs of the top `n` accounts ranked by **total amount successfully withdrawn** (accumulates across all `withdraw` calls). Ties are broken by account ID lexicographically ascending. Accounts with zero withdrawals are excluded. Returns fewer than `n` entries if there are not enough qualifying accounts.

`set_cashback(account_id: str, rate: float) -> None` sets a cashback rate (0.0–1.0) for an account. After this call, each subsequent `deposit` of `amount` credits an extra `amount * rate` to the balance on top of the deposited amount.

### Example

```python
bank = Bank()
for name in ["alice", "bob", "carol"]:
    bank.create_account(name)
    bank.deposit(name, 1000.0)
bank.withdraw("alice", 300.0)
bank.withdraw("bob", 100.0)
bank.withdraw("carol", 200.0)
print(bank.top_spenders(2))   # ['alice', 'carol']

bank.create_account("dave")
bank.set_cashback("dave", 0.1)
bank.deposit("dave", 100.0)
print(bank.get_balance("dave"))  # 110.0  (100 + 10 cashback)
```

levels.description: Add a cashback system where a percentage of each deposit is credited back after a delay.
requirements: All Level 1, 2, and 3 operations still work
flag_suspicious(account_id, threshold) -> bool — flags account if its current balance exceeds threshold; returns True if flagged, False if account not found or balance <= threshold
get_flagged_accounts() -> list[str] — returns sorted list of all flagged account IDs

testCases.input:

bank.create_account("alice")
bank.deposit("alice", 1000.0)

bank = Bank()
bank.create_account("alice")
bank.deposit("alice", 100.0)
print(bank.flag_suspicious("alice", 500.0))

bank = Bank()
bank.create_account("alice")
bank.create_account("bob")
bank.deposit("alice", 1000.0)
bank.flag_suspicious("alice", 500.0)
flagged = bank.get_flagged_accounts()
print("alice" in flagged)

bank = Bank()
bank.create_account("alice")
bank.create_account("bob")
bank.deposit("alice", 1000.0)
bank.flag_suspicious("alice", 500.0)
flagged = bank.get_flagged_accounts()
print("bob" in flagged)

bank = Bank()
print(bank.flag_suspicious("ghost", 100.0))

levels.hint: Keep a `self._flagged: set` in `__init__`. In `flag_suspicious`, check `account_id not in self._accounts` first (return False). Then compare `acc.balance > threshold` — if True, call `self._flagged.add(account_id)` and return True; otherwise return False. In `get_flagged_accounts`, return `sorted(self._flagged)`.
levels.instructions:
## Level 4 — Fraud Detection

Add the ability to flag accounts whose balance exceeds a threshold and retrieve all flagged accounts.

`flag_suspicious(account_id: str, threshold: float) -> bool` checks the current balance of the given account. If the balance is **greater than** `threshold`, the account is added to the flagged set and `True` is returned. Returns `False` if the account does not exist or if its balance does not exceed the threshold.

`get_flagged_accounts() -> list[str]` returns a sorted list of all currently flagged account IDs.

### Example

```python
bank = Bank()
bank.create_account("alice")
bank.create_account("bob")
bank.deposit("alice", 1000.0)
bank.deposit("bob", 100.0)
print(bank.flag_suspicious("alice", 500.0))  # True  (1000 > 500)
print(bank.flag_suspicious("bob", 500.0))    # False (100 <= 500)
print(bank.flag_suspicious("ghost", 100.0))  # False (account not found)
flagged = bank.get_flagged_accounts()
print("alice" in flagged)  # True
print("bob" in flagged)    # False
```

levels.title: Concurrent Transfers
levels.description: Make the banking system thread-safe. Concurrent transfers between accounts must be atomic and deadlock-free.
requirements: All Level 1–4 operations still work

batch_transfer(transfers: list) -> int — executes a list of (from_id, to_id, amount) tuples sequentially; returns count of successful transfers; each transfer is independent (failure does not stop subsequent ones)

testCases.input:
bank = Bank()
for name in ["alice","bob","carol"]:
    bank.create_account(name)
    bank.deposit(name, 500.0)
transfers = [("alice","bob",100.0),("carol","bob",50.0)]
print(bank.batch_transfer(transfers))

bank = Bank()
bank.create_account("alice")
bank.create_account("bob")
bank.deposit("alice", 10.0)
transfers = [("alice","bob",5.0),("alice","bob",100.0)]
print(bank.batch_transfer(transfers))

bank = Bank()
bank.create_account("alice")
bank.deposit("alice", 100.0)
print(bank.batch_transfer([("alice","ghost",50.0)]))

bank = Bank()
print(bank.batch_transfer([]))

bank = Bank()
bank.create_account("alice")
bank.create_account("bob")
bank.deposit("alice", 200.0)
bank.batch_transfer([("alice","bob",75.0)])
print(bank.get_balance("alice"))
print(bank.get_balance("bob"))

levels.hint: In `batch_transfer`, use a simple `for from_id, to_id, amount in transfers:` loop. Call `self.transfer(from_id, to_id, amount)` for each entry and increment a counter when it returns True. Return the counter. No threading needed — sequential execution is correct and simpler.
levels.instructions:
## Level 5 — Batch Transfers

Add the ability to execute a batch of transfers in a single call.

`batch_transfer(transfers: list) -> int` accepts a list of `(from_id, to_id, amount)` tuples and executes them sequentially. Returns the **count of successful transfers**. Each transfer is attempted independently — a failed transfer does not stop subsequent ones. A transfer fails if either account does not exist or the sender has insufficient funds.

### Example

```python
bank = Bank()
for name in ["alice", "bob", "carol"]:
    bank.create_account(name)
    bank.deposit(name, 500.0)

transfers = [("alice", "bob", 100.0), ("carol", "bob", 50.0)]
print(bank.batch_transfer(transfers))  # 2

# Partial failure: alice only has 10.0 after first transfer
bank2 = Bank()
bank2.create_account("alice")
bank2.create_account("bob")
bank2.deposit("alice", 10.0)
transfers2 = [("alice", "bob", 5.0), ("alice", "bob", 100.0)]
print(bank2.batch_transfer(transfers2))  # 1

print(bank.batch_transfer([]))  # 0
```

levels.title: Scheduled Interest & Compound Growth
levels.description: Add interest accrual to savings accounts. Interest compounds at configurable intervals and rates.
requirements: All Level 1–5 operations still work
set_interest_rate(account_id, annual_rate) -> None — sets annual interest rate for account (e.g. 0.05 = 5%); accounts with no rate or rate 0.0 do not accrue interest

accrue_interest(days: int) -> dict — applies compound interest for given days to all accounts with non-zero rate; formula: new_balance = old_balance * (1 + annual_rate/365) ** days; returns {account_id: interest_earned} for affected accounts; returns {} if days <= 0 or no accounts have a rate

testCases.input:
bank = Bank()
bank.create_account("alice")
bank.deposit("alice", 1000.0)
bank.set_interest_rate("alice", 0.365)
result = bank.accrue_interest(1)
print(round(result["alice"], 2))

bank = Bank()
bank.create_account("alice")
bank.create_account("bob")
bank.deposit("alice", 1000.0)
bank.deposit("bob", 2000.0)
bank.set_interest_rate("alice", 0.365)
result = bank.accrue_interest(1)
print("alice" in result)
print("bob" in result)

bank = Bank()
bank.create_account("alice")
bank.deposit("alice", 1000.0)
bank.set_interest_rate("alice", 0.365)
bank.accrue_interest(1)
print(bank.get_balance("alice") > 1000.0)

bank = Bank()
bank.create_account("alice")
bank.deposit("alice", 500.0)
result = bank.accrue_interest(30)
print(result)

bank = Bank()
bank.create_account("alice")
bank.deposit("alice", 1000.0)
bank.set_interest_rate("alice", 0.1)
result = bank.accrue_interest(0)
print(result)

levels.hint: In `set_interest_rate`, store `annual_rate` directly on the account (e.g. `acc.annual_interest_rate = annual_rate`). In `accrue_interest(days)`, return `{}` immediately if `days <= 0`. Then loop over all accounts, skip those with `annual_interest_rate == 0.0`, and compute: `daily_rate = annual_interest_rate / 365`, `new_balance = old_balance * (1 + daily_rate) ** days`, `interest_earned = new_balance - old_balance`. Update `acc.balance = new_balance` and add `account_id: interest_earned` to the result dict.
levels.instructions:
## Level 6 — Scheduled Interest & Compound Growth

Add compound interest accrual to accounts.

`set_interest_rate(account_id: str, annual_rate: float) -> None` sets the annual interest rate for the given account (e.g. `0.05` = 5% per year). Accounts with no rate set (or rate of 0.0) do not accrue interest.

`accrue_interest(days: int) -> dict` applies compound interest for the given number of days to **all accounts that have a non-zero annual rate**. Returns a dict `{account_id: interest_earned}` containing only accounts that had interest applied. Returns `{}` if `days <= 0` or if no accounts have a rate set.

**Formula:** `daily_rate = annual_rate / 365`, `new_balance = old_balance * (1 + daily_rate) ** days`, `interest_earned = new_balance - old_balance`. The balance is updated in place.

### Example

```python
bank = Bank()
bank.create_account("alice")
bank.create_account("bob")
bank.deposit("alice", 1000.0)
bank.deposit("bob", 2000.0)
bank.set_interest_rate("alice", 0.365)  # daily_rate = 0.001

result = bank.accrue_interest(1)
print(round(result["alice"], 2))   # 1.0  (1000 * 0.001)
print("bob" in result)             # False (no rate set)
print(bank.get_balance("alice") > 1000.0)  # True

result2 = bank.accrue_interest(0)
print(result2)                     # {}
```
levels.testFileContent:

    def test_01_print_round_result__alice____2(self):
        """
        print(round(result["alice"], 2))
        Expected: '1.0'
        """
        import io, sys
        captured = io.StringIO()
        sys.stdout = captured
        try:
            bank = Bank()
            bank.create_account("alice")
            bank.deposit("alice", 1000.0)
            bank.set_interest_rate("alice", 0.365)
            result = bank.accrue_interest(1)
            print(round(result["alice"], 2))
        finally:
            sys.stdout = sys.__stdout__

levels.hint: Use `self._cache: OrderedDict[str, str] = OrderedDict()` and `self._capacity = capacity` in `__init__`. In `put`, if the filename is already in the cache, call `self._cache.move_to_end(filename)` then update the value. Otherwise, if `len(self._cache) >= self._capacity`, call `self._cache.popitem(last=False)` to evict the LRU entry, then insert the new entry. In `get`, return `""` immediately if the filename is not in the cache; otherwise call `self._cache.move_to_end(filename)` and return the value.
levels.instructions:
## Level 1 — Basic LRU Cache

You are implementing a Least Recently Used (LRU) file cache. Your class `FileCache` inherits from `FileCacheBase`.

`put(filename: str, content: str) -> None` stores the file content in the cache. If the cache is full (reached `capacity` entries), evict the **least recently used** entry before inserting. If the filename already exists, update its content and mark it as most recently used.

`get(filename: str) -> str` retrieves the file content and marks it as most recently used. Returns `""` if the file is not in the cache.

The cache capacity is set in `__init__` via the `capacity` parameter. Use `collections.OrderedDict` to maintain LRU order efficiently: the LRU entry is at the front (`last=False` in `popitem`), and the MRU entry is at the back.

### Example

```python
cache = FileCache(capacity=2)
cache.put("a.txt", "hello")
cache.put("b.txt", "world")
print(cache.get("a.txt"))   # "hello"  (a.txt is now MRU)
cache.put("c.txt", "new")   # evicts b.txt (LRU)
print(cache.get("b.txt"))   # ""  (evicted)
print(cache.get("c.txt"))   # "new"
```

levels.title: Size Limits & Eviction
levels.description: Add a maximum capacity and LRU (Least Recently Used) eviction policy.
requirements: All Level 1 operations (get, put with LRU eviction) still work
get() calls are now tracked: increment hits on cache hit, misses on cache miss
get_hit_rate() — returns hits / (hits + misses) as float; 0.0 if no get calls made
get_stats() — returns dict with keys: hits, misses, hit_rate, size

testCases.input:

cache.get("x.txt")
cache.get("ghost")

levels.hint: Add `self._hits = 0` and `self._misses = 0` to `__init__`. In `get`, increment `self._hits` on a cache hit and `self._misses` on a miss (before returning `""`). In `get_hit_rate`, return `self._hits / (self._hits + self._misses)` if total > 0 else `0.0`. In `get_stats`, build the dict from those counters plus `len(self._cache)` for size.
levels.instructions:
## Level 2 — Cache Statistics

Extend the Level 1 LRU cache to track cache hits and misses.

Your `FileCache` class still inherits from `FileCacheBase` and accepts `capacity` (entry count) in `__init__`. The `put` and `get` methods work exactly as before, but now `get` must count hits (file found) and misses (file not found) on every call.

`get_hit_rate() -> float` returns the ratio of cache hits to total `get` calls, as a float between 0.0 and 1.0. Returns `0.0` if no `get` calls have been made.

`get_stats() -> dict` returns a dictionary with four keys:
- `hits` — number of successful `get` calls
- `misses` — number of failed `get` calls
- `hit_rate` — same as `get_hit_rate()`
- `size` — current number of entries in the cache

### Example

```python
cache = FileCache(capacity=10)
cache.put("a.txt", "hello")
cache.get("a.txt")      # hit
cache.get("ghost.txt")  # miss
print(cache.get_hit_rate())   # 0.5
stats = cache.get_stats()
print(stats["hits"])    # 1
print(stats["misses"])  # 1
```

levels.title: Byte Size Limits
levels.description: Add a byte-budget put_with_size alongside the count-based put from earlier levels. Use a CacheEntry dataclass to bundle content and size together.
requirements: All Level 1 and 2 operations still work
put_with_size(filename, content, size_bytes) — stores entry using size_bytes as its byte cost
If size_bytes > capacity, silently ignore (do not store)
Evict LRU entries in a loop until total_size + size_bytes <= capacity before inserting
When updating an existing filename, subtract its old byte cost before evicting/re-inserting
get_total_size() — returns total bytes currently in cache

cache.put_with_size("c.txt", "C" * 50, 50)

cache.put_with_size("b.txt", "world", 5)
print(cache.get_total_size())

cache.put_with_size("b.txt", "BBBB", 4)
cache.put_with_size("c.txt", "CCCCCC", 6)
print(cache.get_total_size())

testCases.description: put_with_size update: replacing f.txt with 5-byte version gives total 5
testCases.input:

cache.put_with_size("f.txt", "AB", 2)
cache.put_with_size("f.txt", "ABCDE", 5)
print(cache.get_total_size())

levels.hint: Use `capacity` as the byte budget in `put_with_size`. Before inserting, check `if size_bytes > self._capacity: return`. Remove any existing entry for the same filename (subtract its size from `self._total_size`, delete from `self._cache`). Then evict LRU in a loop: `while self._total_size + size_bytes > self._capacity and self._cache: _, evicted = self._cache.popitem(last=False); self._total_size -= evicted.size`. Finally insert `CacheEntry(content=content, size=size_bytes)` and add to `self._total_size`. `get_total_size()` just returns `self._total_size`.
levels.instructions:
## Level 3 — Size-Based Eviction

Add a second put method that accepts an explicit byte size and enforces a **byte budget**.

Your `FileCache` class still accepts `capacity` in `__init__` (used as the byte budget for `put_with_size`).

`put_with_size(filename: str, content: str, size_bytes: int) -> None` stores the file using the provided `size_bytes` as its cost. Before inserting, evict the least recently used entries until `total_size + size_bytes <= capacity`. If a single item's `size_bytes` exceeds `capacity`, silently ignore it (do not store, return `None`).

When updating an existing filename via `put_with_size`, remove the old entry and its byte cost first, then apply the eviction loop before re-inserting.

`get_total_size() -> int` returns the total number of bytes currently tracked in the cache (from `put_with_size` calls).

The `put` and `get` methods from Level 1 remain available and still use entry-count eviction. Use a `CacheEntry` dataclass with `content: str` and `size: int` fields to store per-entry metadata.

### Example

```python
cache = FileCache(capacity=100)
cache.put_with_size("a.txt", "A" * 30, 30)  # 30 bytes
cache.put_with_size("b.txt", "B" * 30, 30)  # 30 bytes
cache.put_with_size("c.txt", "C" * 50, 50)  # 50 bytes — evicts a.txt then b.txt to make room
print(cache.get("a.txt"))         # "" (evicted)
print(cache.get_total_size())     # 50

cache2 = FileCache(capacity=50)
cache2.put_with_size("huge.txt", "X" * 100, 100)  # 100 > 50, silently ignored
print(cache2.get("huge.txt"))     # ""
```

levels.title: Access Statistics & Reporting

levels.hint: Add `access_count: int = 0` to your `CacheEntry` dataclass. In `get`, after confirming a hit and calling `move_to_end`, do `entry.access_count += 1`. For `get_access_count`, return `self._cache[filename].access_count` if present else `0`. For `top_k_files`, sort with `sorted(self._cache.items(), key=lambda x: (-x[1].access_count, x[0]))` and return the first `k` names.
levels.instructions:
## Level 4 — Access Statistics & Reporting

Track per-file access counts and expose a ranked view of the most-accessed files.

Your `FileCache` class still accepts `capacity` in `__init__`. All prior methods (`put`, `get`, `get_hit_rate`, `get_stats`, `put_with_size`, `get_total_size`) work as before. The `get` method now also increments an `access_count` counter on the stored entry each time the file is retrieved as a hit.

`get_access_count(filename: str) -> int` returns how many times `filename` has been retrieved via a successful `get` call. Returns `0` if the file is not cached or has never been hit.

`top_k_files(k: int) -> list[str]` returns the filenames of the top `k` most accessed files, sorted by access count descending. Ties are broken by filename lexicographically ascending. Returns fewer than `k` entries if there are not enough files in the cache.

### Example

```python
cache = FileCache(capacity=100)
cache.put("a.txt", "hello")
cache.put("b.txt", "world")
cache.get("a.txt")   # hit — access_count("a.txt") = 1
cache.get("a.txt")   # hit — access_count("a.txt") = 2
cache.get("b.txt")   # hit — access_count("b.txt") = 1
print(cache.get_access_count("a.txt"))  # 2
print(cache.top_k_files(1))             # ["a.txt"]
print(cache.top_k_files(2))             # ["a.txt", "b.txt"]
```

levels.title: Thread-Safe Cache
levels.description: Make the file cache safe for concurrent access. Multiple threads may read and write simultaneously without corrupting the LRU order or size accounting.
requirements: All existing operations (get, put, put_with_size, get_stats, get_access_count, top_k_files) are thread-safe via threading.RLock
get_or_load(filename, loader_fn) — returns cached content if present (cache hit); otherwise calls loader_fn() with no arguments, stores the result via put(), and returns it
loader_fn() is called at most once per filename while the entry stays cached
get_or_load triggers normal LRU eviction when it calls put()

levels.hint: In `get_or_load`, first call `val = self.get(filename)`. If `val != ""`, return it immediately (already cached). Otherwise call `content = loader_fn()` (no arguments), then `self.put(filename, content)`, and return `content`. Use `threading.RLock` (already in `__init__`) to protect `put` and `get` individually — `get_or_load` itself does not need an extra lock since it calls the locked methods.
levels.solution:
import threading
from collections import OrderedDict
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class CacheEntry:
    content: str
    size: int
    access_count: int = 0

class FileCache(FileCacheBase):
    def __init__(self, capacity: int = 100):
        self._capacity = capacity  # item count for put(); byte budget for put_with_size()
        self._cache: OrderedDict[str, CacheEntry] = OrderedDict()
        self._total_size: int = 0
        self._hits = 0
        self._misses = 0
        self._lock = threading.RLock()

    def put(self, filename: str, content: str) -> None:
        size = len(content)
        with self._lock:
            if filename in self._cache:
                old = self._cache[filename]
                self._total_size -= old.size
                self._cache.move_to_end(filename)
                self._cache[filename] = CacheEntry(content=content, size=size, access_count=old.access_count)
            else:
                if len(self._cache) >= self._capacity:
                    _, evicted = self._cache.popitem(last=False)
                    self._total_size -= evicted.size
                self._cache[filename] = CacheEntry(content=content, size=size)
            self._total_size += size

    def get(self, filename: str) -> str:
        with self._lock:
            if filename not in self._cache:
                self._misses += 1
                return ""
            self._cache.move_to_end(filename)
            entry = self._cache[filename]
            entry.access_count += 1
            self._hits += 1
            return entry.content

    def get_hit_rate(self) -> float:
        total = self._hits + self._misses
        if total == 0:
            return 0.0
        return self._hits / total

    def get_stats(self) -> dict:
        total = self._hits + self._misses
        hit_rate = self._hits / total if total > 0 else 0.0
        return {
            "hits": self._hits,
            "misses": self._misses,
            "hit_rate": hit_rate,
            "size": len(self._cache),
        }

    def put_with_size(self, filename: str, content: str, size_bytes: int) -> None:
        with self._lock:
            if size_bytes > self._capacity:
                return  # reject items larger than byte capacity
            if filename in self._cache:
                self._total_size -= self._cache[filename].size
                del self._cache[filename]
            while self._total_size + size_bytes > self._capacity and self._cache:
                _, evicted = self._cache.popitem(last=False)
                self._total_size -= evicted.size
            self._cache[filename] = CacheEntry(content=content, size=size_bytes)
            self._total_size += size_bytes

    def get_total_size(self) -> int:
        return self._total_size

    def get_access_count(self, filename: str) -> int:
        with self._lock:
            if filename not in self._cache:
                return 0
            return self._cache[filename].access_count

    def top_k_files(self, k: int) -> list:
        with self._lock:
            ranked = sorted(self._cache.items(), key=lambda x: (-x[1].access_count, x[0]))
            return [name for name, _ in ranked[:k]]

    def get_or_load(self, filename: str, loader_fn) -> str:
        """Return cached content if present; otherwise call loader_fn() to fetch,
        store, and return the content. Thread-safe."""
        val = self.get(filename)
        if val != "":
            return val
        content = loader_fn()
        self.put(filename, content)
        return content
levels.instructions:
## Level 5 — Lazy Loading (get_or_load)

Add a `get_or_load` method that fetches from the cache or calls a loader function to populate it.

Your `FileCache` class still accepts `capacity` in `__init__`. All prior methods work as before. Add a `threading.RLock` to protect all cache operations for thread safety.

`get_or_load(filename: str, loader_fn) -> str` works as follows:
1. If `filename` is in the cache, return its content immediately (this is a cache hit and increments `access_count`).
2. Otherwise, call `loader_fn()` (no arguments) to compute the content, store the result via `put(filename, content)`, and return it.

The loader is called at most once per filename as long as the entry stays in cache. A second `get_or_load` call for the same (still-cached) filename returns the cached value without calling the loader again.

Normal LRU eviction still applies: `get_or_load` can trigger eviction of other entries when it calls `put`.

### Example

```python
calls = []
cache = FileCache(capacity=100)

def loader():
    calls.append(1)
    return "loaded_content"

result = cache.get_or_load("f.txt", loader)
print(result)                       # "loaded_content"
print(cache.get("f.txt"))           # "loaded_content"  (now cached)
cache.get_or_load("f.txt", loader)  # cache hit — loader NOT called again
print(len(calls))                   # 1
```

    def test_05_print_cache_get_access_count__f_txt______1(self):

        finally:

        self.assertEqual(output.strip(), 'True')

levels.title: Multi-Tier Cache
levels.description: Implement a two-tier cache (L1 hot + L2 warm). Entries are promoted from L2 to L1 on access. L1 evictions demote to L2.
requirements: FileCache(l1_capacity, l2_capacity) — two independent LRU caches with separate item-count limits
put(filename, content) — insert into L1 if it has space; if L1 is full, new item goes to L2; evict L2 LRU if L2 is also full
get(filename) — check L1 first (L1 hit: mark MRU, increment access_count); then check L2 (L2 hit: promote to L1, evicting L1 LRU to L2 if needed); return "" on miss
promote(filename) -> bool — move entry from L2 to L1; evict L1 LRU to L2 if L1 full; return False if not in L2
get_tier(filename) -> str — returns "L1", "L2", or "none"
get_tier_stats() -> dict — returns dict with keys: l1_count, l2_count, l1_hits, l2_hits

levels.hint: Use two separate `OrderedDict`s: `self._l1` and `self._l2`, each with a capacity limit. In `put`: if filename already in L1, update and move_to_end; if already in L2, remove from L2; if L1 has room (`len(self._l1) < self._l1_cap`), insert into L1; otherwise insert directly into L2 (evicting L2's LRU if L2 is also full). In `get`: check L1 first (move_to_end, increment l1_hits); then check L2 (pop from L2, insert into L1 — evicting L1's LRU to L2 if needed, increment l2_hits); else return "". In `promote`: `if filename not in self._l2: return False`; pop from L2, evict L1's LRU to L2 if L1 full, insert into L1, return True. `get_tier_stats` returns dict with keys `l1_count`, `l2_count`, `l1_hits`, `l2_hits`.
levels.instructions:
## Level 6 — Multi-Tier Cache (L1 Hot + L2 Warm)

Implement a two-tier cache with entry-count limits for each tier.

The constructor now accepts `l1_capacity` and `l2_capacity` (both item counts):

```python
cache = FileCache(l1_capacity=2, l2_capacity=4)
```

**Placement policy:**
- `put(filename, content)` tries to insert into **L1** first. If L1 already has the file, update it in place (mark as MRU). If L1 has space (under `l1_capacity`), insert into L1. If L1 is full, the new item goes directly to **L2** (no eviction from L1). If L2 is also full, evict L2's LRU item permanently before inserting.
- Items already in L2 are removed from L2 before being placed (into L1 if space, else L2).

**Read policy (`get`):**
- Check L1 first. On L1 hit: mark as MRU in L1, increment `access_count`, return content.
- Check L2 next. On L2 hit: remove from L2, **promote** into L1. If L1 is full, evict L1's LRU to L2 first. Increment `access_count`, return content.
- Return `""` on miss.

**`promote(filename: str) -> bool`** explicitly moves a file from L2 to L1. If L1 is full, evict L1's LRU to L2 first. Returns `False` if the file is not found in L2.

**`get_tier(filename: str) -> str`** returns `"L1"`, `"L2"`, or `"none"`.

**`get_tier_stats() -> dict`** returns a dict with keys: `l1_count`, `l2_count`, `l1_hits`, `l2_hits`.

### Example

```python
cache = FileCache(l1_capacity=1, l2_capacity=2)
cache.put("a.txt", "A")   # L1 has space → a.txt in L1
cache.put("b.txt", "B")   # L1 full → b.txt overflows to L2
print(cache.get_tier("a.txt"))  # "L1"
print(cache.get_tier("b.txt"))  # "L2"
cache.promote("b.txt")
print(cache.get_tier("b.txt"))  # "L1"  (a.txt demoted to L2)
```

import unittest

# This file is auto-generated from the exercise test cases.

class TestLevel6(unittest.TestCase):

    def test_01_print_cache_get_tier__b_txt(self):
        """
        print(cache.get_tier("b.txt"))
        Expected: 'L2\nL1'
        """
        import io, sys
        captured = io.StringIO()
        sys.stdout = captured
        try:
            cache = FileCache(l1_capacity=1, l2_capacity=2)
            cache.put("a.txt", "A")
            cache.put("b.txt", "B")
            print(cache.get_tier("b.txt"))
            cache.promote("b.txt")
            print(cache.get_tier("b.txt"))
        finally:
            sys.stdout = sys.__stdout__
        output = captured.getvalue()
        self.assertEqual(output.strip(), 'L2\nL1')

    def test_02_print_cache_get_tier__a_txt(self):
        """
        print(cache.get_tier("a.txt"))
        Expected: 'L1'
        """
        import io, sys
        captured = io.StringIO()
        sys.stdout = captured
        try:
            cache = FileCache(l1_capacity=2, l2_capacity=4)
            cache.put("a.txt", "A")
            print(cache.get_tier("a.txt"))
        finally:
            sys.stdout = sys.__stdout__
        output = captured.getvalue()
        self.assertEqual(output.strip(), 'L1')

    def test_03_print_cache_get_tier__ghost_txt(self):
        """
        print(cache.get_tier("ghost.txt"))
        Expected: 'none'
        """
        import io, sys
        captured = io.StringIO()
        sys.stdout = captured
        try:
            cache = FileCache(l1_capacity=2, l2_capacity=4)
            print(cache.get_tier("ghost.txt"))
        finally:
            sys.stdout = sys.__stdout__
        output = captured.getvalue()
        self.assertEqual(output.strip(), 'none')

    def test_04_print_cache_promote__ghost_txt(self):
        """
        print(cache.promote("ghost.txt"))
        Expected: 'False'
        """
        import io, sys
        captured = io.StringIO()
        sys.stdout = captured
        try:
            cache = FileCache(l1_capacity=2, l2_capacity=4)
            print(cache.promote("ghost.txt"))
        finally:
            sys.stdout = sys.__stdout__
        output = captured.getvalue()

levels.title: Basic File & Directory Operations
levels.description: Implement `create_file`, `write_file`, `read_file`, `create_dir`, and `exists`. Files and directories live in a flat namespace for now — paths are single-segment names only.
requirements: mkdir(path: str) -> bool — creates directory at path; returns False if already exists
write_file(path: str, content: str) -> None — writes content to file at path; creates or overwrites
read_file(path: str) -> str — returns file content, or "" if path does not exist
delete(path: str) -> bool — removes file or directory; returns False if not found

levels.hint:
Store files as `self._files: dict[str, str]` mapping full absolute path to content string, and directories as `self._dirs: set[str]` initialized with `{"/"}` (root always exists).

For `mkdir(path)`: check if `path` is already in `self._dirs` — return `False` if so. Otherwise `self._dirs.add(path)` and return `True`.

For `write_file(path, content)`: simply assign `self._files[path] = content`. This creates or overwrites the file.

For `read_file(path)`: return `self._files.get(path, "")`.

For `delete(path)`: check `self._files` first — if found, `del self._files[path]` and return `True`. Otherwise return `False`.
levels.instructions:
## Level 1 — Basic File & Directory Operations

You are implementing an in-memory file system. Your class `FileSystem` inherits from `FileSystemBase`.

`mkdir(path: str) -> bool` creates a directory at the given absolute path. Returns `True` on success. Returns `False` if the directory already exists. The root directory `/` always exists.

`write_file(path: str, content: str) -> None` writes `content` to the file at `path`. Creates the file if it does not exist, or overwrites the existing content.

`read_file(path: str) -> str` returns the file content. Returns `""` if the path does not exist or is a directory.

`delete(path: str) -> bool` removes the file or empty directory at `path`. Returns `True` on success, `False` if the path does not exist.

Paths are always absolute (start with `/`). File names and directory names are non-empty strings separated by `/`.

### Example

```python
fs = FileSystem()
print(fs.mkdir("/docs"))               # True
print(fs.mkdir("/docs"))               # False  (already exists)
fs.write_file("/docs/readme.txt", "hello")
print(fs.read_file("/docs/readme.txt"))  # hello
fs.write_file("/notes.txt", "data")
print(fs.read_file("/notes.txt"))      # data
print(fs.delete("/notes.txt"))         # True
print(fs.read_file("/notes.txt"))      # ""
```
levels.testFileContent:

levels.title: Directory Listing & File Metadata
levels.description: Add `list_dir(dir_name)` returning sorted file names, `file_size(dir, file)` returning byte length of content, and `list_all()` returning all `dir/file` paths sorted lexicographically.
requirements: ls(path: str) -> list — returns sorted list of direct child names in directory; [] if path is not a known directory
get_size(path: str) -> int — returns byte length of file content; for directories, total bytes of all files under it recursively; 0 for unknown path
find(root: str, name: str) -> list — returns sorted list of absolute paths under root whose basename equals name; [] if no match

levels.hint:
Store files as `self._files: dict[str, str]` mapping full absolute path to content, and directories as `self._dirs: set[str]` initialized with `{"/"}`.

For `ls(path)`: iterate `self._files` and `self._dirs` looking for entries that start with `path.rstrip("/") + "/"` and have no further `/` in the remaining suffix. Collect the suffix as the child name.

For `get_size(path)`: if `path` in `self._files`, return `len(self._files[path])`. If `path` in `self._dirs`, sum `len(content)` for all files whose path starts with the directory prefix.

For `find(root, name)`: iterate files and dirs; for each, check `entry.rsplit("/", 1)[-1] == name`. Return sorted results.
levels.instructions:
## Level 2 — Directory Listing & File Metadata

Add directory listing and file size support. Your `FileSystem` inherits from `FileSystemBase` and uses absolute paths (always starting with `/`).

`ls(path: str) -> list` returns a sorted list of **names** (not full paths) of direct children (files and directories) of the directory at `path`. Returns `[]` if the path is not a known directory.

`get_size(path: str) -> int` returns the size in bytes (`len(content)`) of a file. For a directory, returns the **total** bytes of all files recursively contained within it. Returns `0` for an unknown path.

`find(root: str, name: str) -> list` returns a sorted list of absolute paths under `root` (including `root` itself) whose **basename** (last component) equals `name`. Returns `[]` if nothing matches.

### Example

```python
fs = FileSystem()
fs.mkdir("/docs")
fs.write_file("/docs/a.txt", "hello")
fs.write_file("/docs/b.txt", "world")
print(fs.ls("/"))         # ["docs"]
print(fs.ls("/docs"))     # ["a.txt", "b.txt"]
print(fs.get_size("/docs/a.txt"))  # 5
print(fs.get_size("/docs"))        # 10
result = fs.find("/", "a.txt")
print(result)             # ["/docs/a.txt"]
```
levels.testFileContent:

levels.title: Move & Copy Operations
levels.description: Add `move_file(src_dir, file_name, dst_dir)` and `copy_file(src_dir, file_name, dst_dir, new_name)`. Move transfers the file (removing from source). Copy duplicates it under a new name. Both return `False` on any conflict or missing entity.
requirements: move(src: str, dst: str) -> bool — moves file or directory from src to dst; returns False if src missing or dst already exists
copy(src: str, dst: str) -> bool — deep-copies file or directory from src to dst; returns False if src missing or dst already exists

testCases.input:
fs = FileSystem()
fs.mkdir("/src")
fs.mkdir("/dst")
fs.write_file("/src/f.txt", "data")
print(fs.move("/src/f.txt", "/dst/f.txt"))
print(fs.read_file("/dst/f.txt"))
print(fs.read_file("/src/f.txt"))

fs = FileSystem()
fs.write_file("/a.txt", "A")
fs.write_file("/b.txt", "B")
print(fs.move("/a.txt", "/b.txt"))

fs = FileSystem()
fs.mkdir("/src")
fs.mkdir("/dst")
fs.write_file("/src/f.txt", "data")
print(fs.copy("/src/f.txt", "/dst/f.txt"))
print(fs.read_file("/src/f.txt"))
print(fs.read_file("/dst/f.txt"))

fs = FileSystem()
fs.write_file("/a.txt", "A")
fs.write_file("/b.txt", "B")
print(fs.copy("/a.txt", "/b.txt"))

fs = FileSystem()
print(fs.move("/ghost.txt", "/dst.txt"))

fs = FileSystem()
print(fs.copy("/ghost.txt", "/dst.txt"))

levels.hint:
For `move(src, dst)`: first check `src` exists in `self._files` or `self._dirs`, and `dst` does NOT exist in either — return `False` otherwise. For files, use `self._files[dst] = self._files.pop(src)`. For directories, discard `src` from `self._dirs`, add `dst`, then rename all entries whose path starts with `src + "/"` by replacing the prefix.

For `copy(src, dst)`: same existence checks, but do NOT remove the source. For files, just assign `self._files[dst] = self._files[src]`. For directories, add `dst` to `self._dirs` and duplicate all child files and sub-dirs.
levels.instructions:
## Level 3 — Move & Copy Operations

Add file and directory move/copy support. Your `FileSystem` inherits from `FileSystemBase`.

`move(src: str, dst: str) -> bool` moves a file or directory from `src` to `dst`. Returns `True` on success. Returns `False` if:
- `src` does not exist, OR
- `dst` already exists (as a file or directory).

When moving a directory, all its contents are moved recursively.

`copy(src: str, dst: str) -> bool` deep-copies a file or directory from `src` to `dst`. Returns `True` on success. Returns `False` under the same conditions as `move` (source must exist, destination must not).

### Example

```python
fs = FileSystem()
fs.mkdir("/src")
fs.mkdir("/dst")
fs.write_file("/src/f.txt", "data")
print(fs.copy("/src/f.txt", "/dst/f.txt"))  # True
print(fs.read_file("/src/f.txt"))           # data  (source preserved)
print(fs.read_file("/dst/f.txt"))           # data
print(fs.move("/src/f.txt", "/dst/f.txt"))  # False (dst already exists)
print(fs.move("/src/f.txt", "/dst/g.txt"))  # True
print(fs.read_file("/src/f.txt"))           # ""    (moved away)
```
levels.testFileContent:

# This file is auto-generated from the exercise test cases.

levels.title: File Permissions & Access Control
levels.description: Add a permission system. Each file has an owner and a mode: `'r'` (read-only), `'rw'` (read-write), or `'private'` (owner only). Add `set_permissions(dir, file, mode)` and update `read_file` and `write_file` to accept a `user` parameter and enforce permissions. Non-owners cannot read `private` files. Non-owners cannot write to `r` or `private` files.
requirements: set_owner(path: str, owner: str) -> None — sets the owner of a file or directory
set_mode(path: str, mode: str) -> None — sets Unix-style permission mode string (e.g. "rwx", "r--", "---"); first char is world-read bit, second is world-write bit
can_read(path: str, user: str) -> bool — returns True if user is owner, or no mode set, or mode[0]=='r'
can_write(path: str, user: str) -> bool — returns True if user is owner, or no mode set, or mode[1]=='w'

testCases.input:
fs = FileSystem()
fs.write_file("/f.txt", "data")
fs.set_owner("/f.txt", "alice")
print(fs.can_read("/f.txt", "alice"))

fs = FileSystem()
fs.write_file("/f.txt", "data")
fs.set_owner("/f.txt", "alice")
fs.set_mode("/f.txt", "r--")
print(fs.can_read("/f.txt", "bob"))

fs = FileSystem()
fs.write_file("/f.txt", "data")
fs.set_owner("/f.txt", "alice")
fs.set_mode("/f.txt", "---")
print(fs.can_read("/f.txt", "bob"))

fs = FileSystem()
fs.write_file("/f.txt", "data")
fs.set_owner("/f.txt", "alice")
fs.set_mode("/f.txt", "r--")
print(fs.can_write("/f.txt", "alice"))

fs = FileSystem()
fs.write_file("/f.txt", "data")
fs.set_owner("/f.txt", "alice")
fs.set_mode("/f.txt", "-w-")
print(fs.can_write("/f.txt", "bob"))

fs = FileSystem()
fs.write_file("/f.txt", "data")
fs.set_owner("/f.txt", "alice")
fs.set_mode("/f.txt", "r--")
print(fs.can_write("/f.txt", "bob"))

fs = FileSystem()
fs.write_file("/f.txt", "data")
print(fs.can_read("/f.txt", "anyone"))

levels.hint:
Store `self._owners: dict[str, str]` (path → owner) and `self._modes: dict[str, str]` (path → mode string like `"rwx"`, `"r--"`, `"---"`).

For `can_read(path, user)`:
1. If `self._owners.get(path) == user` → `True` (owner).
2. If path has no mode set → `True` (default world-readable).
3. Otherwise → `self._modes[path][0] == 'r'`.

For `can_write(path, user)`:
1. If `self._owners.get(path) == user` → `True` (owner).
2. If path has no mode set → `True`.
3. Otherwise → `len(mode) > 1 and self._modes[path][1] == 'w'`.
levels.instructions:
## Level 4 — File Permissions & Access Control

Add Unix-style permission management. Your `FileSystem` inherits from `FileSystemBase`.

`set_owner(path: str, owner: str) -> None` sets the owner of the file or directory at `path`.

`set_mode(path: str, mode: str) -> None` sets the permission mode for `path`. `mode` is a string like `"rwx"`, `"r--"`, `"-w-"`, or `"---"`. The **first** character is the world-read bit (`r`), the **second** is the world-write bit (`w`).

`can_read(path: str, user: str) -> bool` returns `True` if `user` can read `path`. Rules:
- If `user` is the owner → `True` (owner always has full access).
- If no mode is set → `True` (default is world-readable).
- Otherwise → `True` only if `mode[0] == 'r'`.

`can_write(path: str, user: str) -> bool` returns `True` if `user` can write `path`. Rules:
- If `user` is the owner → `True`.
- If no mode is set → `True`.
- Otherwise → `True` only if `mode[1] == 'w'`.

### Example

```python
fs = FileSystem()
fs.write_file("/f.txt", "data")
fs.set_owner("/f.txt", "alice")
fs.set_mode("/f.txt", "r--")
print(fs.can_read("/f.txt", "alice"))   # True  (owner)
print(fs.can_write("/f.txt", "alice"))  # True  (owner)
print(fs.can_read("/f.txt", "bob"))     # True  (mode[0]=='r')
print(fs.can_write("/f.txt", "bob"))    # False (mode[1]=='-')
```

levels.title: Symlinks & Mount Points
levels.description: Add symbolic links and mount points. Symlinks are resolved transparently on every operation.
requirements: symlink(target: str, link_path: str) -> bool — creates symlink at link_path pointing to target; returns False if link_path already exists
resolve(path: str) -> str — resolves symlinks recursively; returns real path; raises ValueError on cycle
mount(mount_point: str, fs_label: str) -> bool — registers fs_label at mount_point (must be existing dir, not already mounted); returns False otherwise
write_file and read_file transparently resolve symlinks before accessing the underlying file

testCases.input:
fs = FileSystem()
fs.write_file("/real.txt", "data")
fs.symlink("/real.txt", "/link.txt")
print(fs.read_file("/link.txt"))

fs = FileSystem()
fs.write_file("/real.txt", "old")
fs.symlink("/real.txt", "/link.txt")
fs.write_file("/link.txt", "new")
print(fs.read_file("/real.txt"))

fs = FileSystem()
fs.write_file("/real.txt", "data")
fs.symlink("/real.txt", "/link.txt")
print(fs.symlink("/real.txt", "/link.txt"))

fs = FileSystem()
fs.write_file("/real.txt", "data")
fs.symlink("/real.txt", "/link.txt")
print(fs.resolve("/link.txt"))

fs = FileSystem()
fs.write_file("/f.txt", "data")
print(fs.resolve("/f.txt"))

fs = FileSystem()
fs.mkdir("/mnt")
print(fs.mount("/mnt", "external_fs"))

fs = FileSystem()
fs.write_file("/f.txt", "data")
print(fs.mount("/f.txt", "external_fs"))

levels.hint:
For `symlink(target, link_path)`: check that `link_path` is not already in `self._files`, `self._dirs`, or `self._symlinks` — return `False` if so. Otherwise, store `self._symlinks[link_path] = target` and return `True`. Note: the baseClass signature is `symlink(target, link_path)` — target comes FIRST.

For `_resolve_path(path, visited=None)`: if `path` is in `self._symlinks`, add it to `visited` (raise `ValueError` if already there) and recurse with the target. Return `path` if it's not a symlink.

For `write_file` and `read_file`: call `self._resolve_path(path)` first, then use the returned real path.

For `mount(mount_point, fs_label)`: return `False` if `mount_point` is not in `self._dirs` or is already in `self._mounts`. Otherwise store `self._mounts[mount_point] = fs_label` and return `True`.
levels.instructions:
## Level 5 — Symlinks & Mount Points

Add symbolic link and mount point support. Your `FileSystem` inherits from `FileSystemBase`.

`symlink(target: str, link_path: str) -> bool` creates a symbolic link at `link_path` pointing to `target`. Returns `False` if `link_path` already exists (as a file, directory, or symlink). All reads and writes to `link_path` transparently operate on `target`.

`resolve(path: str) -> str` follows symlinks recursively and returns the real path. If `path` is not a symlink, returns `path` unchanged. Raises `ValueError("Symlink cycle detected")` on a cycle.

`mount(mount_point: str, fs_label: str) -> bool` registers `fs_label` as mounted at `mount_point`. Returns `False` if `mount_point` is not an existing directory or is already mounted.

**Symlink semantics:** `write_file` and `read_file` must resolve symlinks before accessing the underlying file. This means writing through a symlink updates the target file, not the symlink itself.

### Example

```python
fs = FileSystem()
fs.write_file("/real.txt", "data")
fs.symlink("/real.txt", "/link.txt")
print(fs.read_file("/link.txt"))   # "data"
fs.write_file("/link.txt", "new")
print(fs.read_file("/real.txt"))   # "new"  (write went through symlink)
print(fs.resolve("/link.txt"))     # "/real.txt"
print(fs.resolve("/real.txt"))     # "/real.txt"
fs.mkdir("/mnt")
print(fs.mount("/mnt", "external_fs"))  # True
print(fs.mount("/real.txt", "other"))   # False (not a directory)
```
levels.testFileContent:

            fs = FileSystem()

        finally:

    def test_02_print_fs_read_file___real_txt(self):

        """

        captured = io.StringIO()

        try:
            fs = FileSystem()
            fs.write_file("/real.txt", "old")

    def test_06_print_fs_mount___mnt____external_fs(self):

        finally:

levels.title: Quotas & Audit Log
levels.description: Add per-user disk quotas and a tamper-evident audit log of all file operations.
requirements: set_quota(owner: str, max_bytes: int) -> None — sets maximum total FS bytes; enforced globally across all files
get_usage(owner: str) -> int — returns total bytes of all files owned by owner; 0 if none
get_audit_log(path: str) -> list — returns list of (timestamp, user, operation) tuples for path; [] if no log entries
write_file checks quota before writing; returns False if total FS bytes after write would exceed any set quota
Successful write_file calls append to the per-path audit log

testCases.description: get_usage returns bytes owned by alice (tightened from >= 0 to exact value 5)
testCases.input:
fs = FileSystem()
fs.set_quota("alice", 100)
fs.write_file("/f.txt", "hello")
fs.set_owner("/f.txt", "alice")
print(fs.get_usage("alice"))

testCases.description: quota exceeded: writing 6 bytes when limit is 5 returns False
testCases.input:
fs = FileSystem()
fs.set_quota("alice", 5)
fs.write_file("/f.txt", "hello")
fs.set_owner("/f.txt", "alice")
result = fs.write_file("/g.txt", "world!")
print(result)

testCases.description: get_usage with no files owned by alice returns 0
testCases.input:
fs = FileSystem()
fs.set_quota("alice", 100)
print(fs.get_usage("alice"))

testCases.description: audit log has exactly 1 entry after one write (tightened from len >= 1 to exact len 1)
testCases.input:
fs = FileSystem()
fs.write_file("/f.txt", "data")
log = fs.get_audit_log("/f.txt")
print(len(log))

testCases.description: audit log has exactly 2 entries after two writes (tightened from len >= 2 to exact len 2)
testCases.input:
fs = FileSystem()
fs.write_file("/f.txt", "v1")
fs.write_file("/f.txt", "v2")
log = fs.get_audit_log("/f.txt")
print(len(log))

testCases.description: audit log for unknown path is empty list
testCases.input:
fs = FileSystem()
print(fs.get_audit_log("/ghost.txt"))

levels.hint:
For `set_quota(owner, max_bytes)`: store `self._quotas[owner] = max_bytes`.

For `write_file` quota enforcement: compute `total_before = sum(len(c) for c in self._files.values())` and `total_after = total_before - len(old_content) + len(new_content)`. Then check `for q_limit in self._quotas.values(): if total_after > q_limit: return False`. This enforces a global total byte cap.

For `get_usage(owner)`: return `sum(len(c) for p, c in self._files.items() if self._owners.get(p) == owner)`.

For `get_audit_log(path)`: maintain `self._audit_log: dict[str, list]` mapping path to a list of `(timestamp, user, op)` tuples. Append on every successful `write_file`. Return `list(self._audit_log.get(path, []))`.
levels.instructions:
## Level 6 — Quotas & Audit Log

Add storage quota enforcement and an append-only audit log. Your `FileSystem` inherits from `FileSystemBase`.

`set_quota(owner: str, max_bytes: int) -> None` sets a maximum total byte limit associated with `owner`. Once set, `write_file` will fail (return `False`) if the total bytes stored across **all files in the filesystem** after the write would exceed this limit.

`get_usage(owner: str) -> int` returns the total bytes of all files whose owner (set via `set_owner`) is `owner`. Returns `0` if the owner has no files.

`get_audit_log(path: str) -> list` returns the audit log for `path` as a list of `(timestamp, user, operation)` tuples in chronological order. Returns `[]` if no operations have been recorded for `path`.

**Audit log:** Every call to `write_file` that succeeds appends an entry to the audit log for that path. The log is append-only — entries are never modified or removed.

**Quota enforcement:** `write_file` signature is unchanged (`path, content`). Before writing, compute the new total FS byte count. If it would exceed any quota limit that has been set, return `False` (block the write).

### Example

```python
fs = FileSystem()
fs.set_quota("alice", 10)
fs.write_file("/f.txt", "hello")     # 5 bytes total → ok
fs.set_owner("/f.txt", "alice")
print(fs.get_usage("alice"))         # 5
result = fs.write_file("/g.txt", "world!")  # 11 bytes total > quota 10 → False
print(result)                        # False
log = fs.get_audit_log("/f.txt")
print(len(log) >= 1)                 # True
print(fs.get_audit_log("/ghost.txt"))  # []
```

levels.title: Fixed Window Counter
levels.description: Implement a rate limiter using a fixed time window. `allow_request(user_id, timestamp)` returns `True` if the user has made fewer than `limit` requests in the current window of `window_seconds`. The window resets at multiples of `window_seconds` from epoch (e.g., window=60 means windows are 0–59s, 60–119s, etc.).
requirements: allow_request(user_id, timestamp) — returns True if under limit for current fixed window
Window key = timestamp // window_ms; resets count when window changes
Each user tracked independently

levels.hint: Store `self._state: dict[str, tuple[int, int]]` mapping user_id -> (window_key, count). Window key = `timestamp // window_ms`. In `allow_request`: compute `current_window = timestamp // self._window_ms`. Look up `(stored_window, count) = self._state.get(user_id, (-1, 0))`. If `stored_window != current_window`, reset `count = 0`. If `count >= self._max_requests`, return `False`. Otherwise store `(current_window, count + 1)` and return `True`.
levels.instructions:
## Level 1 — Fixed Window Rate Limiting

You are implementing a rate limiter. Your class `RateLimiter` inherits from `RateLimiterBase`.

`__init__(self, max_requests: int, window_ms: int)` — stores the maximum number of allowed requests per window and the window size in milliseconds.

`allow_request(user_id: str, timestamp: int) -> bool` returns `True` if the request should be allowed, `False` if it should be rejected. The limiter uses a **fixed window** algorithm: each user is allowed at most `max_requests` requests per `window_ms` millisecond window. Windows are aligned to multiples of `window_ms` from epoch 0 (e.g., with `window_ms=1000`, windows are `[0, 999]`, `[1000, 1999]`, etc.). The window key is `timestamp // window_ms`. When the window key changes, the count resets to 0. Each user is tracked independently.

### Example

```python
limiter = RateLimiter(max_requests=3, window_ms=1000)
print(limiter.allow_request("alice", 0))    # True  (1/3)
print(limiter.allow_request("alice", 500))  # True  (2/3)
print(limiter.allow_request("alice", 999))  # True  (3/3)
print(limiter.allow_request("alice", 999))  # False (4/3, limit exceeded)
print(limiter.allow_request("alice", 1000)) # True  (new window)
print(limiter.allow_request("bob", 0))      # True  (separate user)
```

Use a deque to store per-user timestamps.

Use a deque per user to store timestamps; evict timestamps where dq[0] <= timestamp - window_ms before counting
get_usage(user_id, timestamp) -> int — count in-window timestamps without recording a new request

Check `len(dq) < max_requests`, append timestamp, return True. In `get_usage`: same cleanup, but return `len(dq)` without appending.
levels.instructions:
## Level 2 — Sliding Window Rate Limiting

Replace the fixed window algorithm with a **sliding window log** algorithm.

`allow_request(user_id: str, timestamp: int) -> bool` uses a sliding window: count all requests made in the interval `(timestamp - window_ms, timestamp]` (exclusive start, inclusive end). If the count is less than `max_requests`, allow and record the request timestamp. Otherwise, reject. Use a `collections.deque` per user to store request timestamps. On each call, pop timestamps where `dq[0] <= timestamp - window_ms` from the left before counting.

`get_usage(user_id: str, timestamp: int) -> int` returns the count of requests the user has made in the current sliding window at `timestamp`. It applies the same eviction logic as `allow_request` but does not record a new request.

### Example

```python
limiter = RateLimiter(max_requests=3, window_ms=1000)
limiter.allow_request("alice", 100)   # True  (1/3)
limiter.allow_request("alice", 500)   # True  (2/3)
limiter.allow_request("alice", 900)   # True  (3/3)
# At t=1101: cutoff=101, t=100 is evicted — only 500, 900 in window
print(limiter.allow_request("alice", 1101))  # True  (2 in window + new = 3)
print(limiter.allow_request("alice", 1101))  # False (500, 900, 1101 already fill 3)
# At t=1600: cutoff=600, 500 evicted — 900 and 1101 remain
print(limiter.get_usage("alice", 1600))      # 2
```

levels.title: Per-User Custom Limits
levels.description: Extend the sliding window rate limiter with per-user custom limits. Add `set_user_limit(user_id, limit)` to override the global limit for a specific user. If no custom limit is set, the global limit applies. Also add `get_usage(user_id, timestamp)` returning the count of requests in the current window.
requirements: set_user_limit(user_id, limit) — overrides global limit for a specific user
get_user_limit(user_id) -> int — returns custom limit if set, otherwise global max_requests
allow_request uses per-user limit if set, else global default
get_usage(user_id, timestamp) — returns count of requests in current window without adding a new one

testCases.input:
rl = RateLimiter(max_requests=3, window_ms=1000)
rl.set_user_limit("vip", 10)
for i in range(10): rl.allow_request("vip", i)
print(rl.allow_request("vip", 10))

rl = RateLimiter(max_requests=3, window_ms=1000)
rl.set_user_limit("vip", 10)
print(rl.get_user_limit("vip"))

rl = RateLimiter(max_requests=3, window_ms=1000)
print(rl.get_user_limit("unknown"))

rl = RateLimiter(max_requests=2, window_ms=1000)
rl.allow_request("alice", 0)
rl.allow_request("alice", 100)
print(rl.allow_request("alice", 200))

levels.hint: Add `self._custom_limits: dict[str, int] = {}`. In `get_user_limit`: `return self._custom_limits.get(user_id, self._max_requests)`. In `_get_limit` (internal helper): same pattern. In `allow_request`, use `self._get_limit(user_id)` instead of `self._max_requests`. In `get_usage`: copy the deque cleanup logic but don't append — just return `len(dq)`.
levels.instructions:
## Level 3 — Per-User Custom Limits

Add per-user rate limit overrides using a sliding window algorithm.

`set_user_limit(user_id: str, limit: int) -> None` overrides the global `max_requests` for a specific user.

`get_user_limit(user_id: str) -> int` returns the effective limit for the user: the custom limit if set via `set_user_limit`, otherwise the global `max_requests`.

`allow_request` and `get_usage` work the same as Level 2, but `allow_request` now uses `get_user_limit(user_id)` instead of the global `max_requests`.

### Example

```python
limiter = RateLimiter(max_requests=3, window_ms=1000)
limiter.set_user_limit("vip", 10)
print(limiter.get_user_limit("vip"))      # 10
print(limiter.get_user_limit("unknown"))  # 3   (falls back to global)
for i in range(10):
    limiter.allow_request("vip", i)
print(limiter.allow_request("vip", 10))  # False (vip exhausted 10 slots)
```
